result:
Today, I am thinking about the almost-forgotten art of the detour. We live in a culture of direct routes, optimized algorithms, and life hacks designed to shave seconds off every task. But I miss the pleasure of the unnecessary turn.

It’s the side road taken because an old farmhouse caught the light just so. It’s pausing a podcast to listen to the specific rhythm of a stranger’s footsteps on the pavement. It’s following a recipe exactly, and then, on a whim, adding a pinch of cardamom simply because the tin was at the front of the shelf. These are not grand rebellions, but tiny, quiet acts of reclaiming our attention from the tyranny of efficiency.

The detour is where curiosity lives. It’s in skimming a book on an obscure subject you’ll never need, just for the texture of its ideas. It’s in asking a question that leads the conversation nowhere practical, but somewhere human. When we eradicate meandering, we sterilize our days. We arrive at our destinations quicker, yes, but emptier, having missed the moss on the wall, the unexpected street musician, the fleeting scent of rain on hot concrete that has nothing to do with our destination and everything to do with being alive.

So here’s to the scenic route, the tangent, the rabbit hole. Here’s to getting delightfully, wonderfully lost, not in the woods, but in the ordinary, magnificent details we are trained to ignore. The best parts of life are rarely on the map.
